title: Soft salted caramels
subtitle: Cream-and-butter caramels at soft-ball stage
excerpt: Soft, chewy salted caramels made by boiling sugar to soft-ball stage with cream and butter, then setting in a tin and cutting into squares. Sixty caramels from one batch.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: documents the cream-and-butter caramel in the confectionery chapter. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): documents the closely-related Scottish tablet form. The use of flaked salt as a finish is modern but the soft-ball stage and cream-and-butter ratio are pre-1928 standards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap each caramel in baking paper; freeze in a sealed box for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and set in the tin a day ahead; cut and wrap on the day of giving.

recipeTools.notes: Tall and heavy — the cream foams up when added.
20 cm square, lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: The sugar-cooking stage at 115–118°C where the syrup, dropped into ice water, forms a soft pliable ball that flattens between the fingers. Soft-ball gives chewy caramels and the fondant family of confections. Firm-ball (120°C) gives marshmallow; hard-ball (127°C) gives nougat. The exact temperature affects the final texture; a 2°C overshoot turns soft caramels firm and chewy into hard.

content.text: Soft salted caramels are a two-step boil: sugar and golden syrup are first cooked with a little water to a deep amber colour; warm cream and butter are then stirred in, and the whole mixture is cooked to soft-ball stage (118°C). The cooled slab cuts cleanly into 60 squares; a finish of flaked sea salt sits on each one.
The pan needs to be tall — the cream foams up dramatically when it hits the hot sugar. Use a saucepan at least three times the volume of the ingredients to be safe.

attrs.title: Hot syrup is dangerous
attrs.body: The syrup reaches 118°C. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

items.prepNote: 1 teaspoon, plus extra for the top

content.text: Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}} and {{water}} in a tall, heavy saucepan over a low heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves. Stop stirring. Bring to a boil and cook until the syrup turns a deep amber and reads about 165°C on the thermometer — about 10 minutes.

Take the pan off the heat. Pour in the warm {{double-cream}} slowly, with a wooden spoon already in the pan: the mixture will foam dramatically. Whisk in the {{unsalted-butter}} cubes, the {{vanilla-extract}} and the first {{sea-salt-flakes}}. Return to a medium heat and cook, stirring occasionally with a wooden spoon, until the thermometer reads 118°C; about 15 minutes. Pour into the lined tin and leave to set at cool room temperature for 4 hours.

Lift the slab out of the tin. Cut into 60 squares (6 across, 10 down) with an oiled knife. Sprinkle the cut tops with the remaining {{sea-salt-flakes}}. Wrap each caramel in a small square of baking paper or cellophane. Store in an airtight tin.
